reasoning: Indonesian active voice uses me- prefix with assimilation. Before 'b', it becomes mem-. Root is 'buat', so active form is 'membuat'.
Before 'p', the me- prefix becomes mem-. Root is 'pilih', so active form is 'memilih'.
Mandar active voice uses ma- prefix with nasal assimilation. Before 't', it becomes mat-. Root is 'timbe', so active form is 'matimbe'.
Before 'p', the ma- prefix becomes map-. Root is 'pande', so active form is 'mapande'.
In Quechua, possessive suffix after words ending in -m uses -mba instead of -pa. 'hatum' + possessive = 'hatumba'.
Locative suffix after words ending in -m uses -mbi instead of -pi. 'hatum' + locative = 'hatumbi'.
In Zoque, possessive prefix doesn't change words starting with voiceless fricatives. 'flawta' (harmonica) remains 'flawta' (my harmonica).
Before 'd' (already voiced), the possessive adds prenasalization: 'disko' becomes 'ndisko'.
Voiceless 'k' becomes prenasalized voiced stop ŋg. 'kopak' becomes 'ŋgopak' for 'my head'.
In Lunyole, plural prefix before voiceless consonants drops the nasal: olu- becomes e-. 'olusosi' → 'esosi'.
The first blank (n) should be 'Nasal' based on the phonological pattern observed across all five languages.
The second blank (o) should be 'Voiceless Consonant' - all languages avoid nasal + voiceless consonant sequences.
The word 'sentence' contains 'nt' - a nasal (n) directly followed by a voiceless consonant (t), violating the rule.

answer: Indonesian avoids nasal+voiceless consonant sequences through place assimilation of the nasal prefix (mem- before p/b, men- before t/d, meng- before k/g). Mandar similarly uses nasal assimilation, creating geminate consonants (mappasun, mattunu) that avoid heterorganic nasal+voiceless sequences. Quechua inserts a voiced consonant after nasals, changing -pa to -mba and -pi to -mbi after m-final stems. Zoque prenasalizes and voices stops (p→mb, t→nd, k→ŋg) in possessive formation but leaves fricatives unchanged, avoiding nasal+voiceless stop combinations. Lunyole drops the nasal from its prefix before voiceless consonants (olu-→e-) but retains it as en-/em- before voiced consonants.
reasoning: Each language employs a different strategy to avoid nasal+voiceless consonant sequences: assimilation (Indonesian/Mandar), epenthesis (Quechua), prenasalization with voicing (Zoque), or nasal deletion (Lunyole).
